title: Open-Closed Gromov-Witten Invariants of Toric Calabi-Yau 3-Orbifolds
speaker: Chiu-Chu Melissa Liu
abstract: We study open-closed orbifold Gromov-Witten invariants of toric Calabi-Yau 3-orbifolds with respect to Lagrangian branes of Aganagic-Vafa type. We prove an open mirror theorem which expresses generating functions of orbifold disk invariants in terms of Abel-Jacobi maps of the mirror curves. This is a joint work with Bohan Fang and Hsian-Hua Tseng.

proof_stages.label: Toric Calabi-Yau orbifold and orbifold state space
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines toric Calabi-Yau 3-orbifolds through stacky fan and quotient data, then defines the inertia stack, age shift, and Chen-Ruan orbifold cohomology used as the closed A-model state space.

proof_stages.label: GIT quotient chambers and Kähler parameters
proof_stages.summary: Every such orbifold is presented as a symplectic/GIT quotient by a connected torus, with charge vectors, chambers, and complexified Kähler parameters that will enter the generating functions and mirror map.

proof_stages.label: Aganagic-Vafa branes and framing on the A-side
proof_stages.summary: The Lagrangian boundary conditions are described in local quotient charts, their first homology and monodromy data are identified, and a one-dimensional torus framing is chosen for localization.

proof_stages.label: Mirror hypersurface and framed mirror curve
proof_stages.summary: From the toric charges, the chosen brane, and the framing, the B-model mirror is constructed as a noncompact Calabi-Yau hypersurface together with an affine mirror curve carrying open and closed moduli.

proof_stages.label: Open orbifold Gromov-Witten invariants
proof_stages.summary: The relevant moduli spaces of bordered orbifold stable maps are specified by genus, boundary classes, degree, and insertions, and the open-closed invariants and generating functions are defined by framed torus localization.

proof_stages.label: Complex-valued disk potential
proof_stages.summary: Specializing to the disk sector, the orbifold-valued generating function is paired with a framing-dependent character of the stabilizer to produce the complex-valued A-model disk potential to be compared with the B-model.

proof_stages.label: Open-closed mirror theorem and proof mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The theorem states that, after explicit open and closed mirror maps, the A-model disk potential equals the logarithmic/period expansion on the mirror curve, with proof sketched via localization, the closed mirror theorem, winding summation, and extended Picard-Fuchs equations.

examples.name: Basic toric fan for \(\mathbb C^3\)
examples.note: The speaker uses the unit simplex fan for \(\mathbb C^3\) to make the stacky-fan-to-quotient recipe concrete; it illustrates the trivial-kernel smooth case rather than generating a new idea.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes the fan vectors for the bb C^3 example, forms the associated torus map, computes that its kernel is trivial, and concludes the toric variety is bb C^3.

passes.reason: the [31mbasic[0m fan for \(\mathbb C^3\) is the minimal single unimodular cone with trivial quotient, used only as the simplest illustrative toric Calabi–Yau case
the fan is the standard single unimodular cone for affine C^3, a minimal-volume maximally regular base case used only illustratively
The fan is the single unimodular cone for affine \(\mathbb C^3\) with trivial quotient data, used as the minimal illustrative toric Calabi–Yau example rather than to refute anything.
The fan is the single unimodular standard cone for smooth affine \(\mathbb C^3\), a minimal/trivial toric Calabi–Yau base case used only illustratively.
the standard one-cone unimodular fan for \(\mathbb C^3\) has trivial quotient and minimal lattice index/triangle area, and is used only as the simplest illustration
The fan is the single unimodular cone for affine \(\mathbb C^3\), a minimal smooth base case with trivial quotient group and minimal lattice volume, used only as an illustrative example.
It is the standard single unimodular coordinate-simplex cone with trivial quotient, the minimal smooth affine toric Calabi–Yau 3-fold fan used only as an illustrative base example.
It is used as the simplest illustrative toric Calabi–Yau chart: the trivial single smooth unimodular cone for C^3, with minimal lattice volume and no quotient kernel, not to refute a claim.
Used as the simplest illustrative toric Calabi–Yau example; its fan is the single unimodular cone for affine space, a minimal/smooth determinant-one case rather than an obstruction.
Used only as the simplest illustrative toric Calabi–Yau example; its fan is the minimal smooth unimodular 3-cone with trivial quotient group.
The standard single unimodular coordinate cone for \(\mathbb C^3\) is a minimal/trivial-kernel smooth toric base case, not used adversarially.
The fan of \(\mathbb C^3\) is the standard single unimodular coordinate cone, used only as the minimal smooth base example with maximal ray-permutation regularity rather than to refute a claim.
